Subject: Share your research knowledge with us
Body: Annals of Case Reports IF: 4.99*| Pubmed Indexing Dear Doctor, Greetings for the day! This is a reminder mail, as we did not receive any response from you to our previous emails. Please spare your time to read this mail, as we value your high-quality paper submissions for our journal. Annals of case Reports is a platform for your innovative research work. We wonder if you are encouraging our journal “Annals of Case Reports” and submit your valuable and worthy stuff to us. We accept all types of manuscripts like research, review, case report, editorial, etc. for our upcoming issue. ***Video Articles also accepted for this upcoming Issue*** Please submit your articles on or before April 25, 2025 to this mail directly. Note**: If the article is ready, kindly submit it quickly to this mail for a Discounted Fee. Each article will undergo a fast-track review process and publish within 3-6 days. You can submit the articles in online submission system and the articles will be peer-reviewed and tracking systems help in conducting quality and quick review processing. We promise to serve you better in always. Awaiting your rapid come back. Sincerely, Tricia Editorial Assistant ISSN: 2574-7754

Subject: Bonsai for hotel or office buildings
Body: Dear Sir or Madam, Thank you for your e-mail or inquiry or interest. Please noted that we are specialized for the artificial plants and flowers and the vertical garden and big trees and succulents and so on. And we also have the different flowers mixed for the flower wall, can you check from the website for more than 2000 items there? If you would like to visit us and need an invitation we would like very much to make the invitation letter for you! Please visit our Canton Fair booths or our New big showroom which is more than 1000 square meters. We have customers from more than 120 countries. Most of them are the top 3 of the relative business. Yes, of course we can send you samples before you place an order, and also we can send you the e-catalogue or price list with photos, and the MOQ for this kind of flower wall is 10 square meters. For the small bonsai or artificial plants MOQ just 3 or 5 cartons each is ok! Thank you! We will be waiting for your earlier reply and hope to have the chance to cooperate with you in the near future, we are sure that we can cooperate with each other smoothly and successfully, because we have been doing so in the past years! And we ensure you that we supply you the good quality goods with very reasonable prices and very fast delivery. And we hope that you will be our partners and to be a basement area to expand and supply our goods to your other cities around your shop or warehouse or your store either on-line or off line. If you have agent or warehouse in China, we can send the less container loading(LCL) order to your other suppliers and combine a full container loading order, or we can arrange the LCL order to be a Full container loading (LCL)order and load for you. Either LCL or FCL order both ok for us. Thank you! -- -- Thank you and Best regards ------------------ Mr. Harold Xie Sales Manager of Dongguan Jinchen Artware Co., Ltd. Subject: Meta Fined $1.3 Billion
Body: Forbes NewslettersPlus: How This Fearless Fund Beats The MarketADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesGood morning, To mark this weekend's World Bee Day, we go inside a billionaire bee colony and explore how warming climates may impact the busy pollinators. The sting heard around the world comes from Ireland's Data Protection Commission, which has fined Facebook's owner, Meta, a record $1.3 billion for mishandling user data. Have a great day, Caroline Howard Executive Editor Follow me on Twitter and Forbes.comBREAKING NEWSGETTY IMAGESFacebook’s parent Meta has been ordered to pay a record $1.3 billion (€1.2 billion) fine by the European Union for failing to adhere to the bloc’s stringent privacy rules, in the latest severe financial penalty handed to an American tech giant by the EU.Credit Suisse staff are gearing up to sue Switzerland’s financial regulator over hundreds of millions of dollars worth of bonuses that were canceled after the bank was hastily rescued by rival UBS earlier this year, according to reports, adding to the deal’s already sizable legal fallout as the two banks work to finalize the merger.BUSINESS + FINANCEBitcoin prices have swung wildly over the last few yearsFORBES DIGITAL ASSETSBitcoin has stormed into 2023 but failed to regain its 2021 momentum—though one high-profile investor thinks that could be about to change. Ahead of what could be a game-changing bitcoin pivot, Jack Mallers, CEO of bitcoin payments company Strike, says the company will expand its reach to around 3 billion people across 65 countries—challenging critics and competitors: "Who's laughing now?"After Bud Light came under fire from the far-right for a brief partnership with trans TikTok star Dylan Mulvaney, critics seem to be piling on more companies for ad campaigns that reach out to the LGBTQ community, in some cases recirculating marketing campaigns that are years old—including Jack Daniels, Nike and Ford—branding the companies as “woke” and calling for boycotts.WEALTH + ENTREPRENEURSHIPILLUSTRATION BY YUNJIA YUAN FOR FORBES; GETTY IMAGESAmerica’s richest farmers, billionaires Stewart and Lynda Resnick, run Wonderful Co., with $5 billion in annual sales from products such as Halos, Fiji Water, Pom Wonderful and seedless lemons. Wonderful’s roughly 80,000 hives, with about 4.5 billion bees, are among a handful of honey bee farms responsible for pollinating a third of Americans’ food. But the real buzz is how they rent them out.We have exclusive investment advice from a Vietnam veteran who turned a two-bedroom house into a $4.7 billion fortune. Arte Moreno, who made his first billion in billboards and now owns the L.A. Angels, shares his investment failures and triumphs—and why there’s no such thing as owning too much real estate.MONEY + POLITICSPresident Joe Biden cautioned that congressional Republicans might do something “outrageous” to force the U.S. to default on its loans, as the negotiations between congressional Republicans and the White House over whether to raise the debt ceiling remain uncertain and a June 1 deadline to avert a default looms.Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis said there is “zero” chance the state will back down from its efforts to curb Disney’s power, taking a defiant stance in his first public comments after the company canceled a $1 billion office project in Florida. The political war with the expected 2024 GOP presidential nominee began when Disney denounced the state’s “Don’t Say Gay” law.SPORTS + ENTERTAINMENTNETFLIXSquid Game returns to Netflix in November, though there will be no murders this time. Squid Game: The Challenge is a reality show spinoff of the hit Korean dystopian drama where some 450 contestants will compete in various non-lethal games to win a grand total of $4.5 million.Adidas will start selling some of its remaining Yeezy products at the end of this month—one of the final moves in the company’s dissociation with rapper Kanye West following a string of antisemitic comments he made last year. The existing Yeezy products still held by Adidas are said to be worth $1.3 billion.Daily Cover Story Marshfield's three portfolio managers STEPHEN VOSS FOR FORBESPick Your Poison: How This Fearless Fund Beats The MarketREAD ARTICLETOPLINEWhen Chipotle Mexican Grill came under fire in the fall of 2015 for making hundreds of diners sick at several locations across the U.S., customers—and investors—couldn’t flee fast enough. The popular fast food chain’s stock plunged 34% in the last quarter of the year.“They poisoned people, and everybody stopped going there,” says Chris Niemczewski, founder and managing principal of Washington, D.C.-based Marshfield Associates. “Actually I stopped going there—for about a week.”The stock fell an additional 40% in 2016 and 2017, and Niemczewski used the prolonged drought to load up on what everyone was selling. Chipotle stock soared more than fourfold over the next three years, making Marshfield several times its money before it cashed out by the end of 2020.It’s a prime example of Marshfield’s high-conviction approach to investing against the grain that Niemczewski has implemented since he founded the firm in 1989. Marshfield now manages $4.7 billion in assets—$470 million in its lone mutual fund, the Marshfield Concentrated Opportunity Fund, with the rest in separately managed accounts that follow the same strategy. WHY IT MATTERS“We have a broad funnel—we’ll talk to anything that piques somebody’s interest—but we have a really, really narrow spout at the end of the funnel, and very few ideas get through,” says co-portfolio manager Elise Hoffmann, who has been at Marshfield since 1995. “We'd rather commit a crime of missing out on a company rather than buying something, a crime of omission rather than commission.”Niemczewski and Hoffmann, who married 15 years ago, added former minor league baseball announcer Chad Goldberg, who has worked for the firm since 2008, as a third portfolio manager last year. Marshfield’s research team of six operates by consensus. Adding heft to the collective decision: all the principals are required to have their personal equity portfolios entirely invested in the strategy.MORELegendary Investor Strategies And Stocks For The Current MarketADVERTISEMENTFACTS AND COMMENTSCarrying pollen is a real workout. A new study shows that the body temperature of bumble bees heats up as the amount of pollen they carry increases—and this could lead to heat stress as the global climate continues to warm.41.3°- 48.4°CDegrees common eastern bumble bees appear to overheat +2°CBody warmth of bees carting a full load of pollen vs. unladen bees Heavy LoadBees can ferry up to 80% of their body weight in pollenSTRATEGY AND SUCCESSSelling a home for the first time can be a bewildering experience. Reality television has glamorized and oversimplified the process—everything is quick and easy, and homes often sell for more than the list price. If you want to avoid unnecessary real estate surprises and headaches, here are some first-time seller mistakes to avoid. Subject: Re: [opensuse] 10.3 and powermanagement
Body: On Thursday 07 February 2008 07:23:52 am Aaron Kulkis wrote: > John E. Perry wrote: > > As I mentioned in yet another thread, hp wants me to dump suse and > > restore the machine to its original xp pro configuration, then they'll > > work with me to find out if my hardware problems (no sound, either under > > xp or suse, bad readings from kde, power switch dead) can be fixed under > > warranty. > > For this reason (and only this reason), I pull the Windows > drive, and buy a different drive to put SuSE on. > > When the computer needs repair..the original drive with > the Windows installation goes back in solely to please > the techs. > FWIW, I had an HP dv6000 with hardware problems, and when I made the support call I did tell them I was running linux though I resisted any suggestions to try Windows and forced them to diagnose it anyways. When I got my ticket to send it in for service, I pulled the drive altogether and placed a sticky on it saying "HDD removed for corporate policy reasons". They fixed my system and sent it back three days later. If you have a hardware problem, don't bother including the drive. They can troubleshoot it without it, and it keeps them from sending it back saying "WON'T FIX" because you have linux on it. Just my 2c... Cheers, KV

Subject: House lawmakers vote on the debt ceiling; the Sackler family gets immunity; the Pittsburgh shooting trial is underway
Body: Plus, tech experts have a dire warning about AI. View this email online by Suzanne Nuyen May 31, 2023 Good morning. I've got some bad news for the people looking forward to peach season: 90% of this year's Georgia's peach crop has been destroyed by bad weather and warming climate. 😢 Here's what else we're following today.House lawmakers are set to vote tonight on a bill that would lift the U.S. debt ceiling and avoid a default. While President Biden and House Speaker Kevin McCarthy see the deal as a needed compromise, both conservative and progressive lawmakers are unhappy. Alex Brandon/AP 🎧 NPR's Deirdre Walsh says on Up First that some Democrats are upset about policy provisions in the bill that would change how energy projects are approved and add work requirements for some adults who receive assistance like food stamps. If the bill passes, it will head to the Senate. Walsh reports that Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer said he would keep the Senate late for the vote. ➡️ Republicans upset with the deal are threatening to oust McCarthy. Texas Gov. Chip Roy said no Republicans should vote for the bill and threatened a "reckoning" no matter what happens. A New York federal appeals court's ruling in a controversial bankruptcy deal will grant the Sackler family immunity from lawsuits related to its role in the opioid crisis. The family will pay roughly $6 billion for addiction and health care programs and give up control of their company, Purdue Pharma. 🎧 While this is a legal victory for the Sackler family, NPR's Briann Mann reports that it's a different story for its reputation. While the family used to be respected philanthropists, protests over its role in pushing OxyContin sales have led many universities and museums to remove their names from galleries. Lawyers gave their opening statements yesterday in the trial of Robert Bowers, the man accused of shooting and killing 11 Jewish worshippers at a Pittsburgh synagogue in 2018. Bowers faces 63 federal charges, including hate crimes, and could get the death penalty if found guilty. 🎧 WESA's Oliver Morrison reports that prosecutors emphasized the antisemitic statements Bowers made online. Meanwhile, the defense isn't disputing Bowers' guilt but will challenge whether he was rational. One of the witnesses, Rabbi Jeffrey Myers, testified and described hiding in a bathroom and reciting a Jewish prayer for the congregants. ➡️ Keep up with the trial with WESA's live blog. Tech industry experts have a dire warning: The AI programs we're creating could outsmart us and lead to our collective demise. A group that includes scientists, AI experts, journalists and policymakers has released a statement urging the world to give the same priority to mitigating AI risks as it would to pandemics or nuclear war. 🎧 On Morning Edition, University of Massachusetts Amherst associate professor Scott Niekum discusses what persuaded him to sign the warning. Newsletter continues after sponsor message Arin YoonPhotographer Arin Yoon came to the U.S. when she was 5 years old. She writes about the Korean concept of han, or the "collective feeling of sorrow relating to having been colonized and oppressed." Her photos re-examine her connection to the U.S., reconsidering histories while exploring her connection to the landscape, her children and their past and future selves. Sam Yeh/AFP via Getty ImagesDecades ago, Taiwan set up propaganda broadcast stations off the coast of mainland China to try and get listeners in Communist China to defect. Women's voices and the dulcet tones of pop star Teresa Teng became one of their key tools of persuasion. 🎧 Listen to the Taiwanese women behind the microphones reflect on their "fight for hearts and minds" or read their stories. Ron Harris/APFormer first lady Rosalynn Carter, a longtime advocate for mental health care, has been diagnosed with dementia. A statement from The Carter Center says she "continues to live happily at home with her husband," Jimmy Carter, who entered hospice care in February.It's bird migration season. If you listen carefully, stunning and diverse birdsong can be found even in the densest cities.Air New Zealand will begin weighing passengers for international flights "for safety reasons." Passengers won't see numbers from the scales, which will be anonymous. Listen to your local NPR station. Visit NPR.org to hear live radio from KUNC (edit station).
